devanagari: पृषध्र कवि करूष धृष्ट नृग नरिष्यंतादिष्टानां वंशविस्तारः । श्रीशुक उवाच । एवं गतेऽथ सुद्युम्ने मनुर्वैवस्वतः सुते । पुत्रकामः तपस्तेपे यम...
english_devnagari: pṛṣadhra kavi karūṣa dhṛṣṭa nṛga nariṣyaṃtādiṣṭānāṃ vaṃśavistāraḥśrīśuka uvācaevaṃ gate'tha sudyumne manurvaivasvataḥ suteputrakāmaḥ tapastepe yamunāyāṃ śataṃ samāḥ
translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
When his son Sudyumna thus left (for the forest), Manu, the son of Vivasvat, being desirous of begetting a son performed penance for a hundred years, on the bank of the Yamunā.

devanagari: ततोऽयजन् मनुर्देवं अपत्यार्थं हरिं प्रभुम् । इक्ष्वाकु पूर्वजान् पुत्रान् लेभे स्वसदृशान् दश ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: tato'yajan manurdevaṃ apatyārthaṃ hariṃ prabhumikṣvāku pūrvajān putrān lebhe svasadṛśān daśa
translation: Thereafter he worshipped Lord Hari (by performing a sacrifice) for (male) progeny; and he had ten sons, Ikṣvāku being the eldest, all resembling himself.

devanagari: पृषध्रस्तु मनोः पुत्रो गोपालो गुरुणा कृतः । पालयामास गा यत्तो रात्र्यां वीरासनव्रतः ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: pṛṣadhrastu manoḥ putro gopālo guruṇā kṛtaḥpālayāmāsa gā yatto rātryāṃ vīrāsanavrataḥ
translation: Manu’s (eighth) son, Pṛṣadhra, was entrusted with the duty of protecting the cows, by his preceptor (as Vasiṣṭha found him competent to tend the bulls). Vigilantly he tended the cows at night, remaining alert in the posture called Vīrāsana (kneeling on one knee).

devanagari: एकदा प्राविशद् गोष्ठं शार्दूलो निशि वर्षति । शयाना गाव उत्थाय भीतास्ता बभ्रमुर्व्रजे ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: ekadā prāviśad goṣṭhaṃ śārdūlo niśi varṣatiśayānā gāva utthāya bhītāstā babhramurvraje
translation: On one occasion, while it was raining at night, a tiger made its way into the cowpen. Cows which were lying there, got panicky and springing to their feet, ran about in that enclosure.

devanagari: एकां जग्राह बलवान् सा चुक्रोश भयातुरा । तस्यास्तु क्रन्दितं श्रुत्वा पृषध्रोऽनुससार ह ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: ekāṃ jagrāha balavān sā cukrośa bhayāturātasyāstu kranditaṃ śrutvā pṛṣadhro'nusasāra ha
translation: The powerful tiger seized one of the cows, and she, overwhelmed with fear, screamed loudly. It is reported that when Pṛṣadhra heard that screaming in agony, he rushed in haste in that direction, with a sword in hand. In the darkness of night, when even stars were invisible due to clouds, he unwittingly chopped off the head of a tawny coloured cow, believing it to be a tiger.

devanagari: खड्गमादाय तरसा प्रलीनोडुगणे निशि । अजानन् अहनद् बभ्रोः शिरः शार्दूलशङ्कया ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: khaḍgamādāya tarasā pralīnoḍugaṇe niśiajānan ahanad babhroḥ śiraḥ śārdūlaśaṅkayā
translation: The powerful tiger seized one of the cows, and she, overwhelmed with fear, screamed loudly. It is reported that when Pṛṣadhra heard that screaming in agony, he rushed in haste in that direction, with a sword in hand. In the darkness of night, when even stars were invisible due to clouds, he unwittingly chopped off the head of a tawny coloured cow, believing it to be a tiger.

devanagari: व्याघ्रोऽपि वृक्णश्रवणो निस्त्रिंशाग्राहतस्ततः । निश्चक्राम भृशं भीतो रक्तं पथि समुत्सृजन् ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: vyāghro'pi vṛkṇaśravaṇo nistriṃśāgrāhatastataḥniścakrāma bhṛśaṃ bhīto raktaṃ pathi samutsṛjan
translation: The tiger too got its ear severed with the end of the sword in that stroke, but escaped in extreme panic, dripping blood all the way.

devanagari: मन्यमानो हतं व्याघ्रं पृषध्रः परवीरहा । अद्राक्षीत् स्वहतां बभ्रुं व्युष्टायां निशि दुःखितः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: manyamāno hataṃ vyāghraṃ pṛṣadhraḥ paravīrahāadrākṣīt svahatāṃ babhruṃ vyuṣṭāyāṃ niśi duḥkhitaḥ
translation: Pṛṣadhra, the destroyer of inimical warriors, who believed that the tiger had been killed, found, at the close of night, to his sore grief, that a reddish-brown cow had been killed by him, in the (darkness of) night.

devanagari: तं शशाप कुलाचार्यः कृतागसं अकामतः । न क्षत्रबन्धुः शूद्रस्त्वं कर्मणा भवितामुना ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ śaśāpa kulācāryaḥ kṛtāgasaṃ akāmataḥna kṣatrabandhuḥ śūdrastvaṃ karmaṇā bhavitāmunā
translation: To Pṛṣadhra who has unknowingly committed the sin killing a cow, Vasiṣṭha, the family preceptor (instead of advising him the expiatory rite or procedure for such unwitting acts) pronounced the following imprecation: “In consequence of this (thoughtless) act, you shall be not even the vilest of Kṣattriyas but a veritable Śūdra.”

devanagari: एवं शप्तस्तु गुरुणा प्रत्यगृह्णात् कृताञ्जलिः । अधारयद् व्रतं वीर ऊर्ध्वरेता मुनिप्रियम् ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ śaptastu guruṇā pratyagṛhṇāt kṛtāñjaliḥadhārayad vrataṃ vīra ūrdhvaretā munipriyam
translation: When cursed in this way by the family preceptor, the great warrior accepted it (respectfully) with folded palms. He observed the vow of life-long celibacy—a vow so esteemed by sages.

devanagari: वासुदेवे भगवति सर्वात्मनि परेऽमले । एकान्तित्वं गतो भक्त्या सर्वभूतसुहृत् समः ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: vāsudeve bhagavati sarvātmani pare'maleekāntitvaṃ gato bhaktyā sarvabhūtasuhṛt samaḥ
translation: Being exclusively and absolutely devoted to the glorious Lord Vāsudeva, the absolutely pure (blemishless), transcendental, universal Spirit, he became a friend and a well-wisher of all beings, impartial to all.

devanagari: विमुक्तसंगः शान्तात्मा संयताक्षोऽपरिग्रहः । यदृच्छयोपपन्नेन कल्पयन् वृत्तिमात्मनः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: vimuktasaṃgaḥ śāntātmā saṃyatākṣo'parigrahaḥyadṛcchayopapannena kalpayan vṛttimātmanaḥ
translation: Completely devoid of attachment, with a serene mind, and with all senses under full control, bereft of all possessions, maintaining himself on whatever came to him by chance (without asking for it), fixing his mind in the Supreme Soul, satiated in spiritual knowledge[1], and absorbed in meditation, he roamed about the earth, presenting an appearance of a blind and deaf dunce.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
v.l. jñāna-hṛṣṭa—Delighted in spiritual knowledge.

devanagari: आत्मनि आत्मानमाधाय ज्ञानतृप्तः समाहितः । विचचार महीमेतां जडान्धबधिराकृतिः ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: ātmani ātmānamādhāya jñānatṛptaḥ samāhitaḥvicacāra mahīmetāṃ jaḍāndhabadhirākṛtiḥ
translation: Completely devoid of attachment, with a serene mind, and with all senses under full control, bereft of all possessions, maintaining himself on whatever came to him by chance (without asking for it), fixing his mind in the Supreme Soul, satiated in spiritual knowledge[1], and absorbed in meditation, he roamed about the earth, presenting an appearance of a blind and deaf dunce.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
v.l. jñāna-hṛṣṭa—Delighted in spiritual knowledge.

devanagari: एवं वृत्तो वनं गत्वा दृष्ट्वा दावाग्निमुत्थितम् । तेनोपयुक्तकरणो ब्रह्म प्राप परं मुनिः ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ vṛtto vanaṃ gatvā dṛṣṭvā dāvāgnimutthitamtenopayuktakaraṇo brahma prāpa paraṃ muniḥ
translation: Leading this way of life, he happened to enter a forest where, seeing the outbreak of forest-conflagration, he silently allowed his body to be consumed by it and became one with the transcendental Brahman.

devanagari: कविः कनीयान् विषयेषु निःस्पृहो । विसृज्य राज्यं सह बन्धुभिर्वनम् । निवेश्य चित्ते पुरुषं स्वरोचिषं । विवेश कैशोरवयाः परं गतः ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: kaviḥ kanīyān viṣayeṣu niḥspṛhovisṛjya rājyaṃ saha bandhubhirvanamniveśya citte puruṣaṃ svarociṣaṃviveśa kaiśoravayāḥ paraṃ gataḥ
translation: And the younger son Kavi[2] (even as a child) had no desire for worldly objects. Renouncing the kingdom along with the relatives, he enthroned the self-effulgent Supreme Person in his heart and repairing to the forest, even in his boyhood, ultimately attained to the Supreme Brahman.
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Padaratnāvalī takes Kavi ‘wise’ as the adj. qualifying Pṛṣadhra and treats this verse as a further elucidation of the above (12-13) verses. (uktameva vivṛṇoti).

devanagari: करूषान् मानवाद् आसन् कारूषाः क्षत्रजातयः । उत्तरापथगोप्तारो ब्रह्मण्या धर्मवत्सलाः ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: karūṣān mānavād āsan kārūṣāḥ kṣatrajātayaḥuttarāpathagoptāro brahmaṇyā dharmavatsalāḥ
translation: From Karūṣa, the son of Manu, was descended the Kṣatriya clan called Kārūṣas who ruled over the northern regions, were friendly to Brāhmaṇas and devoted to righteousness.

devanagari: धृष्टाद् धार्ष्टमभूत् क्षत्रं ब्रह्मभूयं गतं क्षितौ । नृगस्य वंशः सुमतिः भूतज्योतिः ततो वसुः ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: dhṛṣṭād dhārṣṭamabhūt kṣatraṃ brahmabhūyaṃ gataṃ kṣitaunṛgasya vaṃśaḥ sumatiḥ bhūtajyotiḥ tato vasuḥ
translation: The Kṣatriya clan called Dhārṣṭa was born from Dhṛṣṭa but attained Brāhmaṇahood (Brahmanical virtues and way of life) on the earth. In the race of Nṛga, Nṛga had a son Sumati whose son was Bhūtajyoti to whom was born a son, Vasu.

devanagari: वसोः प्रतीकस्तत्पुत्र ओघवान् ओघवत्पिता । कन्या चौघवती नाम सुदर्शन उवाह ताम् ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: vasoḥ pratīkastatputra oghavān oghavatpitākanyā caughavatī nāma sudarśana uvāha tām
translation: Vasu had a son Pratīka whose son Oghavān was the father of a son called Oghavat and a daughter named Oghavatī whom Sudarśana married.

devanagari: चित्रसेनो नरिष्यन्ताद् ऋक्षस्तस्य सुतोऽभवत् । तस्य मीढ्वांस्ततः पूर्ण इन्द्रसेनस्तु तत्सुतः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: citraseno nariṣyantād ṛkṣastasya suto'bhavattasya mīḍhvāṃstataḥ pūrṇa indrasenastu tatsutaḥ
translation: Citrasena who was born from Nariṣyanta had a son called Dakṣa. Dakṣa’s son was Mīḍhvān of whom was born Kūrca who had a son called Indrasena.

devanagari: वीतिहोत्रस्तु इन्द्रसेनात् तस्य सत्यश्रवा अभूत् । उरुश्रवाः सुतस्तस्य देवदत्तस्ततोऽभवत् ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: vītihotrastu indrasenāt tasya satyaśravā abhūturuśravāḥ sutastasya devadattastato'bhavat
translation: Indrasena’s son was Vītihotra who got a son named Satyaśravas who, on his part, had Uruśravasas his son. Devadatta was born from Uruśravas.

devanagari: ततोऽग्निवेश्यो भगवान् अग्निः स्वयं अभूत्सुतः । कानीन इति विख्यातो जातूकर्ण्यो महान् ऋषिः ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: tato'gniveśyo bhagavān agniḥ svayaṃ abhūtsutaḥkānīna iti vikhyāto jātūkarṇyo mahān ṛṣiḥ
translation: The venerable fire-god Agni himself was born as a son of Uruśravas, and was called Agniveśya. He became famous as a great sage Kānīna or Jātukarṇya.

devanagari: ततो ब्रह्मकुलं जातं आग्निवेश्यायनं नृप । नरिष्यन्तान्वयः प्रोक्तो दिष्टवंशमतः शृणु ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: tato brahmakulaṃ jātaṃ āgniveśyāyanaṃ nṛpanariṣyantānvayaḥ prokto diṣṭavaṃśamataḥ śṛṇu
translation: From Agniveśya descended a Brāhmaṇa clan known as Agniveśyāyana, Oh king. The race of Nariṣyanta has been thus described in details. Now listen to the line of Diṣṭa.

devanagari: नाभागो दिष्टपुत्रोऽन्यः कर्मणा वैश्यतां गतः । भलन्दनः सुतस्तस्य वत्सप्रीतिः भलन्दनात् ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: nābhāgo diṣṭaputro'nyaḥ karmaṇā vaiśyatāṃ gataḥbhalandanaḥ sutastasya vatsaprītiḥ bhalandanāt
translation: Nābhāga, another son of Diṣṭa (different from the one to be described later[3]) became a Vaiśya by following the profession (agriculture, cow-tending etc.) of that class. His son was Bhalandana whose son was Vatsaprīti.

devanagari: वत्सप्रीतेः सुतः प्रांशुः तत्सुतं प्रमतिं विदुः । खनित्रः प्रमतेः तस्मात् चाक्षुषोऽथ विविंशतिः ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: vatsaprīteḥ sutaḥ prāṃśuḥ tatsutaṃ pramatiṃ viduḥkhanitraḥ pramateḥ tasmāt cākṣuṣo'tha viviṃśatiḥ
translation: Prāṃśu was the son of Vatsaprīti. Prāṃśu’s son is well-known as Pramati; whose son was Khanitra[4], Khanitra’s son was Cākṣuṣa, the father of Viviṃśati.

devanagari: विविंशतेः सुतो रंभः खनीनेत्रोऽस्य धार्मिकः । करन्धमो महाराज तस्यासीद् आत्मजो नृप ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: viviṃśateḥ suto raṃbhaḥ khanīnetro'sya dhārmikaḥkarandhamo mahārāja tasyāsīd ātmajo nṛpa
translation: Rambha[5] was the son of Viviṃśati. Rambha’s son was the religious Khaninetra, and Karandhama was Khaninetra’s son, Oh great king.

devanagari: तस्य आवीक्षित् सुतो यस्य मरुत्तः चक्रवर्ति अभूत् । संवर्तोऽयाजयद् यं वै महायोग्यङ्गिरःसुतः ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: tasya āvīkṣit suto yasya maruttaḥ cakravarti abhūtsaṃvarto'yājayad yaṃ vai mahāyogyaṅgiraḥsutaḥ
translation: His son was Avīkṣit. His son Marutta became a sovereign of the whole of the globe of the earth—the same sovereign for whom the great yogin, Saṃvartta, the son of sage Aṅgiras, performed sacrifices.

devanagari: मरुत्तस्य यथा यज्ञो न तथान्योऽस्ति कश्चन । सर्वं हिरण्मयं त्वासीत् यत्किञ्चिच्चास्य शोभनम् ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: maruttasya yathā yajño na tathānyo'sti kaścanasarvaṃ hiraṇmayaṃ tvāsīt yatkiñciccāsya śobhanam
translation: Nobody else has ever performed a sacrifice so gloriously as did Marutta. All the utensils therein were of pure gold, and whatever other materials were used in his sacrifice, were auspicious and beautiful.

devanagari: अमाद्यदिन्द्रः सोमेन दक्षिणाभिर्द्विजातयः । मरुतः परिवेष्टारो विश्वेदेवाः सभासदः ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: amādyadindraḥ somena dakṣiṇābhirdvijātayaḥmarutaḥ pariveṣṭāro viśvedevāḥ sabhāsadaḥ
translation: Indra (drank so much Soma in that sacrifice that he) became inebriated and Brāhmaṇas were oversatisfied with the gifts (dakṣiṇā). Marut-gods served food (in that sacrifice), and the whole fraternity of gods (Viśvedevāḥ) were the members of the sacrificial assembly.[6]
purport:
Footnote [6]:
This is nothing but an echo of:
marutaḥ pariveṣṭāro maruttasyāvasan gṛhe /
āvikṣitasya kāma-prer viśvedevāḥ sabhāsada iti //
  —Ait. Br. 8.21.14, Śat. Br. 13.5.4.6. Mahābhārata 12.29.18.
The same is repeated in Mahābhārata Droṇa 55.43-44.

devanagari: मरुत्तस्य दमः पुत्रः तस्यासीद् राज्यवर्धनः । सुधृतिस्तत्सुतो जज्ञे सौधृतेयो नरः सुतः ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: maruttasya damaḥ putraḥ tasyāsīd rājyavardhanaḥsudhṛtistatsuto jajñe saudhṛteyo naraḥ sutaḥ
translation: Marutta had a son called Dama whose son was Rājyavardhana; his son was Sudhṛti and Nara was born as a son of Sudhṛti.

devanagari: तत्सुतः केवलः तस्माद् बन्धुमान् वेगवान् ततः । बुधस्तस्याभवद् यस्य तृणबिन्दुर्महीपतिः ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: tatsutaḥ kevalaḥ tasmād bandhumān vegavān tataḥbudhastasyābhavad yasya tṛṇabindurmahīpatiḥ
translation: His son was Kevala whose son was Bandhumān of whom was born Vegavān. Bandhu was his (Vegavān’s) son. Of Bandhu was born the king of the earth Tṛṇabindu.

devanagari: तं भेजेऽलम्बुषा देवी भजनीयगुणालयम् । वराप्सरा यतः पुत्राः कन्या च इडविडाभवत् ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ bheje'lambuṣā devī bhajanīyaguṇālayamvarāpsarā yataḥ putrāḥ kanyā ca iḍaviḍābhavat
translation: Being an abode of praiseworthy qualities, a prominent heavenly damsel Alambuṣā resorted to him (as his wife), and bore him a number of sons and a daughter known as Iḍaviḍā (v.l. Ilavilā).

devanagari: यस्यां उत्पादयामास विश्रवा धनदं सुतम् । प्रादाय विद्यां परमां ऋषिर्योगेश्वरः पितुः ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: yasyāṃ utpādayāmāsa viśravā dhanadaṃ sutamprādāya vidyāṃ paramāṃ ṛṣiryogeśvaraḥ pituḥ
translation: The sage Viśravas begot on her a son known as Kubera (the donor of wealth). From his father who was a master of yogas, Kubera received the Supreme Lore (about the soul).

devanagari: विशालः शून्यबन्धुश्च धूम्रकेतुश्च तत्सुताः । विशालो वंशकृद् राजा वैशालीं निर्ममे पुरीम् ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: viśālaḥ śūnyabandhuśca dhūmraketuśca tatsutāḥviśālo vaṃśakṛd rājā vaiśālīṃ nirmame purīm
translation: The sons of this king were Viśāla, Śūnyabandhu, and Dhūmraketu. Visāla was the founder of a dynasty and built the city of Vaiśālī.

devanagari: हेमचन्द्रः सुतस्तस्य धूम्राक्षः तस्य चात्मजः । तत्पुत्रात् संयमादासीत् कृशाश्वः सहदेवजः ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: hemacandraḥ sutastasya dhūmrākṣaḥ tasya cātmajaḥtatputrāt saṃyamādāsīt kṛśāśvaḥ sahadevajaḥ
translation: His (Viśāla’s) son was Hemacandra, whose son was Dhūmrākṣa. From his son Saṃyama were born Kṛśāśva and Devaja[7].
purport:
Footnote [7]:
But according to Bhāgavata Candrikā & Padaratnāvalī Dhūmrākṣa’s son was Sahadeva and Kṛśāśva was Sahadeva’s son.

devanagari: कृशाश्वात् सोमदत्तोऽभूद् योऽश्वमेधैः इडस्पतिम् । इष्ट्वा पुरुषमापाग्र्यां गतिं योगेश्वराश्रिताम् ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: kṛśāśvāt somadatto'bhūd yo'śvamedhaiḥ iḍaspatimiṣṭvā puruṣamāpāgryāṃ gatiṃ yogeśvarāśritām
translation: From Kṛśāśva was born Somadatta who, by performing horse-sacrifices, worshipped the Supreme Person, the presiding deity of sacrifices[8] and attained to the foremost (highest goal through the support of the master of yoga.

devanagari: सौमदत्तिस्तु सुमतिः तत्सुतो जनमेजयः । एते वैशालभूपालाः तृणबिन्दोर्यशोधराः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: saumadattistu sumatiḥ tatsuto janamejayaḥete vaiśālabhūpālāḥ tṛṇabindoryaśodharāḥ
translation:
Somadatta’s son was Sumati, whose son was Janmejaya. These rulers of the kingdom of Vaiśāli held up the glory (glorious tradition of Tṛṇabindu).
